summary: Gruber reviews the iPad Air 2 as a breakthrough device that surpasses the iPhone 6 in performance and approaches MacBook Air territory, while the iPad Mini 3 receives only Touch ID and a gold option. He argues the Air 2's A8X chip, 2 GB RAM, improved display, thinner design, and Metal API mark a turning point where performance is no longer a reason to choose a MacBook Air over an iPad. Despite this, Gruber personally prefers the Mini form factor for his reading-heavy usage. He criticizes Apple's 16 GB storage tier as undermining the company's brand promise of only making good products, and notes that Apple's in-house chip design is pulling ahead of the entire industry.
key_points: The iPad Air 2's A8X chip with 3 cores and 2 GB RAM makes it faster than the iPhone 6 and a 2011 MacBook Air, marking the iPad's shift from following the iPhone to leading in performance
The iPad Mini 3 is essentially unchanged from the Mini 2 aside from Touch ID and a gold option, suggesting Apple sees the Mini as a consumption device and the Air as a MacBook alternative
Apple's Metal API combined with the A8X's GPU performance puts the iPad Air 2 on potentially even footing with the MacBook Air for graphics-intensive tasks
The 16 GB base storage tier undermines Apple's brand promise of only making good products and should be replaced with 32 GB as the starting tier
Apple's in-house chip design team is single-handedly pulling away from the rest of the industry in computing performance and efficiency
money_quotes: In short, I don't think performance is any longer a reason to buy a MacBook Air instead of an iPad Air. The choice comes down to form factor and personal preference. This marks a turning point.
This year, all previous patterns are busted.
The iPad is no longer following in the wake of the iPhone, performance- and specs-wise. It's forging ahead.
It really does feel like we're getting close to just holding a piece of glass.
16 GB iPads work against the foundation of Apple's brand, which is that they only make good products.
Now, with these 16 GB entry-level devices, it's more like 'Are you sure?', 'Better', and 'Best'.
At the moment, it looks like one company, Apple, is single-handedly pulling away from the entire rest of the industry in terms of computing performance and efficiency.
Using the camera, in conjunction with smart software, as an instructional aid is something else: brand-new territory. Something only a tablet is suited for.
key_insight: The iPad Air 2 marks a turning point where the iPad surpasses the iPhone in performance and approaches laptop-class computing, driven by Apple's in-house chip design pulling ahead of the entire industry.
